questions.question:
Derive the time complexity of the following algorithm using step-counting method:
```
ALGORITHM MystAlgo(n)
 sum ← 0
 for i ← 1 to n do
 for j ← 1 to i² do
 sum ← sum + 1
 return sum
```
keyPoints: Identify outer loop runs n times
Inner loop bound is i², depends on i
Set up summation: Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ i²
Apply formula for sum of squares: n(n+1)(2n+1)/6
Simplify: ≈ n³/3 for large n
Time complexity: Θ(n³)

questions.question:
Analyze the following algorithm and express its time complexity in Θ-notation:
```
i ← n
while i ≥ 1 do
 for j ← 1 to n do
 print(j)
 i ← ⌊i/3⌋
```
keyPoints: Identify outer loop: halving from n gives log₃n iterations
Inner loop runs n times independent of i
Total operations: n × log₃n
Apply change of base for logarithms: log₃n = Θ(log n)
Final complexity: Θ(n log n)

questions.question: Explain the general plan for analyzing non-recursive algorithms. Why is identifying the 'basic operation' critical in this process?
keyPoints: Six steps: Decide parameter n, identify basic operation, determine frequency, set up summation, solve summation, express asymptotically
Basic operation is the operation contributing most to running time
It determines the efficiency class of the algorithm
All other operations are considered constant overhead
Selecting wrong basic operation leads to incorrect analysis

questions.question: Calculate the exact number of key comparisons in Sequential Search in (i) worst case, (ii) best case, and (iii) average case assuming the key is present with equal probability at any position.
keyPoints: Worst case: n comparisons (key at last position or not found)
Best case: 1 comparison (key at first position)
Express as Θ(n) for worst and average, Θ(1) for best
Average case assumes uniform distribution

questions.question:
What is the time complexity of an algorithm with the following operation counts? Prove your answer.
C(n) = 5n³ + 10n² + 3n + 100
keyPoints: Apply asymptotic dominance rule
Identify highest order term: 5n³
Drop constants: 5n³ becomes n³
Justify: For large n, n³ term dominates lower terms
Therefore: Θ(n³) - cubic complexity
Proof: lim(n→∞) C(n)/n³ = 5, a constant

questions.question:
Analyze the following nested loop structure and derive Θ-notation:
```
for i ← 1 to n do
 for j ← 1 to i do
 for k ← 1 to j do
 x ← x + 1
```
keyPoints: Innermost loop runs j times for each j value
Middle loop: for each i, sum over j from 1 to i
Set up: Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ Σⱼ₌₁ⁱ j
Apply formula: Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ [i(i+1)/2] = Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ (i²+i)/2
Split summation: ½Σi² + ½Σi = ½[n(n+1)(2n+1)/6] + ½[n(n+1)/2]
Simplify to: n³/6 + n²/2 + n/3 = Θ(n³)

questions.question: Differentiate between Best Case, Worst Case, and Average Case analysis. For Sequential Search, derive all three complexities.
keyPoints: Best Case: Minimum running time over all inputs of size n
Worst Case: Maximum running time over all inputs of size n
Average Case: Expected running time assuming probability distribution
Sequential Search Best: O(1) when key at first position
Sequential Search Worst: O(n) when key at last or absent
Sequential Search Average: O(n/2) = O(n) when key equally likely at any position

questions.question: Provide a rigorous mathematical analysis of Bubble Sort to determine its time complexity in best, worst, and average cases. Use the step-counting method with summations.
keyPoints: Basic operation: comparison of two adjacent elements
Worst Case: Reverse sorted array - every pair must be swapped
Derivation: Pass i makes (n-1-i) comparisons, total = Σᵢ₌₀ⁿ⁻² (n-1-i) = n(n-1)/2 = Θ(n²)
Best Case: Already sorted - single pass with no swaps, (n-1) comparisons = Θ(n)
Average Case: Approximately half elements out of place, expected comparisons ≈ n(n-1)/4 = Θ(n²)
Proof using summation: arithmetic series solution required
Conclusion: Worst/Average Θ(n²), Best Θ(n)

questions.question:
Analyze the following algorithm completely. Determine exact count, set up summation, solve it, and express in asymptotic notation:
```
ALGORITHM ComplexAnalysis(n)
 count ← 0
 for i ← 1 to n do
 j ← i
 while j ≤ n do
 count ← count + 1
 j ← 2*j
 return count
```
keyPoints: Outer loop runs n times
Inner while loop: j doubles each iteration starting from i
For fixed i, iterations = ⌊log₂(n/i)⌋ + 1
Total count = Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ (⌊log₂(n/i)⌋ + 1)
This is complex to solve exactly - use approximation
Upper bound: for each i, max log₂n iterations, so ≤ nlog₂n = O(n log n)
Lower bound: for i ≤ n/2, at least log₂2 = 1 iteration, giving Ω(n)
Tight bound: The sum approximates n log n - 1.5n for large n
Result: Θ(n log n)

questions.question: Derive the time complexity for Selection Sort using mathematical analysis. Prove that it makes exactly n(n-1)/2 comparisons regardless of input arrangement.
keyPoints: Selection Sort: Find minimum in unsorted portion, swap with first element
Pass 1: Compare n-1 elements to find minimum of n elements
Pass 2: Compare n-2 elements for remaining n-1 elements
Continue until only 1 element remains
Total comparisons = (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1
Sum of arithmetic series: Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ⁻¹ i = (n-1)n/2
Simplify: n(n-1)/2 = (n²-n)/2 = Θ(n²)
Proof by induction or direct summation formula
Key insight: Same count for best, worst, average cases - input order doesn't affect comparison count
Time complexity: Θ(n²)
